speaker: interviewer
text: You were telling me about your arrival in Theresien.

speaker: interviewee
text: I forgot to mention, the reason I gave to the Gestapo and to the SS why I want to join-- I had to proclaim Eva for my bri-- for-- for my bride, and I simply put her into a tender stant-- state of pregnancy. She was pregnant with me. And that's why I considered it fair to join her. When we moved, then, to this huge kind of waiting space where Eva was with her mother, and I was explaining how it came about that I am there, she really believed that Eva is pregnant with me. And she believed it literally until [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] she, poor thing, went to the gas chambers, I think. Anyhow, there we were, out of these barracks, and Eva was put into a building where-- where live the young women. At that point, one can say girls, for they had been about 15, 16, 17 years old. They were about 16 together in one room. We mentioned the space. And there were triple bunks, and they get along marvellously. They all were Zionist kids.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where were you living?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was living at that point, for a relatively short time, in one of the main military barracks, where there were about 4,000 or 5,000 men living together, and we were in triple bunks in different sections of that huge building. And from there, I got myself out and about in, I would say, four to five months. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I was working, at that point, in the agriculture. It was simply we worked on the fields outside of the ghetto. We were cultivating cucumbers and tomatoes. The harvest, naturally, didn't belong to Jews. The harvest belonged to the SS man. And we were watched and guarded from above, and we had special kinds of passes to pass outside of Theresien. And I became a foreman, and I had a pass with-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I think at that point it was still my name-- plus 14 other Jews. And I moved at that point. We found a space. We have-- were four young guys, and we found a space directly in the walls of this big fortress. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] It's a horse stables, and the horse hands were living there in rooms which would be, I mean, about three by four square-- three-- three by four meters, with one huge window leading into a hall that all was-- was in the walls of-- of the fortress. But it was outside of the ghetto itself, but not-- still inside of the guarded circle [INAUDIBLE] by the gendarmes, so we didn't need any special passes. We had to chisel a window so that it would-- all the conditions of the department of health, which was there in the ghetto, would-- we would comply with it. And we were four young men. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Two of them went with me all through, through Auschwitz and through Schwarzheide and the death march. And the fourth one went through a different routing. The tragedy of this, which I want to mention, is that they all survived. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And all three of my friends, one of them here in Montreal, committed suicide. They all died by hanging them-- them-- themselves. Egon, E-G-O-N, did it in Prague about six years after the war. My friend [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] George [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] hanged himself in the year 1982. And my friend George Wassermann, W-A-S-S-E-R-M-A-N-N, hanged himself in Australia in 1960.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I'm a real survivor. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I wrote a paper on it as an explanation, what probably happened-- happened to them. But that's a sad memory.

speaker: interviewee
text: Anyhow, there we lived quite nicely. We have organized all the furnishing and everything what we had in it, from the trains which are standing in front of us, which were taking the Jewish furniture and furnishings into Germany, which we simply opened during the night. And we somehow accomplished something very unusual for Theresienstadt. I used--

speaker: interviewer
text: What were you risking by doing that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Being hanged. And I used the words, for the first time, the words organized. In the camp survivor's slang, or in the camp slang, organization means stealing. It means you couldn't steal only so easily. You could, but you usually had to do it in presence of somebody else, or being in a group which would help you to achieve your goal, whatever it was, whether it was to bring some of the vegetable into the ghetto, or whether it was having to do this. There I stayed literally up to the year 1943, where-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and I found a space in one of these relatively tall buildings for Theresien, which was next to the building which was already forbidden for us. There was a barrier, and that was where the-- the gendarmes had their headquarters. We called it the casino. And in the loft of that building, there was space, and I built there something which was called in French a [SPEAKING FRENCH]. You had to go through the roof, build a window and a room behind that, and I had to bring in some heat, which was kind of a small iron stove. And I had to build it from the scratch, from the floor, the walls, the door, the kind of primitive insulation, and the window. I mean, the wood [INAUDIBLE], whatever else was there, was organized. And it was very good for-- it was very unusual, for I could stay there, which was strictly forbidden, with Eva together. And from there, we left Theresien.

speaker: interviewer
text: Dr. Roden, what was a typical day like in Theresien?

speaker: interviewee
text: It depends what you were doing. You know, there was a drastic difference between the young people, who could cope with it somehow, and the old people, who were completely out of their normal routine and simply-- they were hungry, and they were undercl-- underclothed, and they were cold. And they were-- they lost all their privacy and all their human dignity. Put together, I mean, into one room, they put-- where we were four, into that room, they would put two dozens of old people, who were just slowly preparing themselves to die, who were doing some kind of menial job, whether it was cleaning the street, or, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I don't know, preparing something in the distribution or in the registration or anything. As long as they could work, they could get some kind of better food, or a little bit more. There were queues and queues for food which was distributed. And compared to other things, this still wasn't so bad. But we didn't know that. But then we slowly started to realize that the Germans are preparing something else. They are grooming this ghetto for probably something that they want to show to the world. In other words, want to show to the international and Swiss Red Cross how well, actually, are the Jews being taking care of. And they-- we were already left. But half a year after we left there-- filmed there a film, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] which was called 'Hitler gibt der Juden eine Stadt,' Hitler is giving the Jews a city. And the whole Theresienstadt, all Theresien was painted and everything was arranged there. I mean, there was a coffee shop and a restaurant, and there were orchestras playing, and the children were thanking to the SS men for enabling them doing this and that. And while we were still in Theresien, it was obvious that one is preparing something like this. Yet every week, a transport was leaving. And with every transport, as we were taken out from this daily transport, we automatically fell into the transport, meaning Eva and I, and our parents, who came later then. But we were specifically in every transport. We were always put out on some reason, which was arranged at the council of elders. And finally, we were called by Edelstein [INAUDIBLE] and who told us, kids, you both are very likable. I can understand that it will sound to you very absurd. But I cannot protect you. We cannot protect you as bridegroom and bride. You have to get married.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we said, so we'll get married. So the next-- I think the next-- the day after this, two days later, we were married. We were married in a room which the Germans specifically left for that. One has to realize that families did not live-- live together. Men lived with men, women lived with women, and similar.

speaker: interviewee
text: And the contact was forbidden, yet [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] this was a ghetto. Everything was possible in the ghetto. And the Germans, they agreed. People even get-- can get married. They had a special room allotted for that in the local insane asylum. There were plenty of people who were brought from all over Bohemia and Moravia who were insane, so one room was simply given for the marriages, with a little bit of German tongue in cheek. And we were married under a chuppah by a rabbi with all the trimmings, and with stamping on the glass and saying mazel tov. And then simply, with a passkey which I made, we spent the-- the night with Eva on top of the building where she lived with the 16 plus, with-- and every room other-- other girls. There was plenty of hay there in the loft. Everything was fine. Only we discovered that it was just loaded with fleas-- thousands and thousands of fleas. So it was about 3:00 or 4:00 in the morning and we ran. One gets accustomed to bedbugs. One gets accustomed to lice. But fleas, at least me, no. Eva is very resistant.

speaker: interviewer
text: Dr. Roden, on what day did you get married?

speaker: interviewee
text: We got married on the 22 of November, 1942, so that in 1982, we had a special kind of-- 1992, we had a special kind of anniversary. I know people who were born on 1922, also people who were killed on 1920-- November-- No-- November 22. Jack Kennedy.

speaker: interviewer
text: When-- Do you remember the date that you actually arrived in Theresien?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was beginning of-- of-- of-- of October, 1942.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were married a month after you had arrived in Theresien?

speaker: interviewee
text: Uh-huh. By that time, we had been in four transports. Once I had to pretend that I had got typhoid fever. I injected myself with a typhoid fever vaccine. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] In the-- the year 1943, so I worked in the agriculture. Then I-- when I moved to this loft, so I switched to transport-- transportation-- which was, I mean, a kind of commando, which occasionally went for supplies outside of the ghetto and brought supplies, or the train would come and we had to unload it very quickly, or we had to transfer all kinds of stuff which was seized from the Jews in the protectorate and transfer it somewhere else. It had the tremendous advantage that, simply, we were young, and we had access to any amount of food, and any amount, practically, any amount of clothing, also, which was very unusual. We were altogether in this about 60 people.

speaker: interviewer
text: You mentioned that, for most other residents of Theresien, they were dependent on standing in long queues to get--

speaker: interviewee
text: The normal ration for a working person, working Jew, was between 800 and 900 calories. For a nonworking Jew, less than 600 calories, which is just enough to die. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] The young people took it much better. Eva was working, for instance, in a home for young children. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And we were quite, relatively, well fed. And the medical care was good for them. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] They all, with exception of enormously small quantity, they all died eventually, being transper-- transported to the east. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And we-- then in 1942, we have been, the whole families-- Eva's parents, my parents and me-- we have been in three different transports, and we succeeded to get ourself out. But then, in December, again we were all in it. And I simply said to Eva, it has no sense. Eventually we have to go, so at least we'll go all together. Her father came to Theresien from the prison, and so we went. We left Theresien in December 22, 1943. We didn't know exactly where we are going. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Just before we left, a little vignette. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I'm standing there with Eva in this loft, and we already have got some kind of a small suitcase with us, and a rucksack. And I said to her, you have got this ring, which was strictly forbidden, naturally, with a relatively big diamond. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] You'll never carry it through. It might cause your destruction. Give me the thing. And I kneeled down, and I put-- as I build the floor, so it was on 2x2s-- and I went in between as far as I could go, and on a s-- flat piece of wood, I placed the ring under the floorboard. And we left. We all went into the same wagon. There have been wagons. One can see it in the Holocaust Museum in Washington. It was originally for eight horses or 40 soldiers, or in this case, about 150 or 200 Jews, packed together like sardines. We took off, and we went all through the day. We knew that we are passing through Prague, for one could see through a small window things, and we passed through northern Bohemia. And finally, in the morning, early in the morning-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] no, it was at night, sorry.

speaker: interviewer
text: We stopped and we were-- the person who was at this little window with barbed wire on it, there was standing a-- a-- a railway-- a railway man at the platform of a station with a little light there. And we were hearing, where are we? And he said to us in Polish, you? You are in Auschwitz. And he did this.

speaker: interviewee
text: And in that moment, the doors opened. And the commando simply, who had something to do with getting the Jews out as quickly as possible, started. There was the yelling, there was the dogs, and then a kind of catastrophic noise, and out, out, out, and on the trucks. We didn't know how lucky we were at that point. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] We were taken to a camp in Auschwitz-Birkenau, B-I-R-K-E-N-A-U, Auschwitz zwei, 2, in something which was called the family camp. Obviously, the Germans repeating the same story, trying to have something to show to the world. I use the word Potemkin's village to show simply how, even in the most absurd circumstances, when they have got at least 200,000 or 250,000 Jewish workers working in the surrounding factories, which all began-- belonged to huge German companies, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] that even there, they are being handled in certain kind of-- according to the Geneva Convention. [PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS] There I started to form my basic rules of survival, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] which helped, probably, many people, but which I had to break many times and kept me alive, so I don't know whether they are valid. When I was lecturing zillions of years after, so I had five rules of survival. First one was that you shall never be a hero. You shall never volunteer to anything. Third one, you shall be lucky. The fourth one, you shall be really lucky. In other words, you shall have 'mazel,' we are going. And number five, maybe, maybe, some of us are either lucky or they've got something that they will pick up a situation intuitively that they will do the right thing in the right moment at the right place. As I mentioned, I had to break the rule several times. But there we were lucky with Eva that [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] we went to Theresien, and from Theresien we went to this family camp, which was a showpiece compared to anything else. We landed there out of the trucks, and there was 5,000 people who left Theresien three months before us, a transport which left in September. And we were just gasping for-- for air, for they had, some of them, these striped uniforms, which was quite OK prepared, probably to anything. But they acted like-- like animals, like beasts. You know, when they came there themselves, they-- they are instructed by mostly the Polish capo, meaning from the Italian capo, means the head, C-A-P-O, that you have to be tough, you have to be brutal, you have to be really something. Then the SS men will leave you in peace and let you survive. And they tried it on us. I met there somebody who was the head of block 14, who was a lawyer whom I knew from the Zionist movement, who had seen me and he picked me to be his servant, which was tremendously lucky move at that point. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] the block filled with men, guys, maybe about 500, 700 of us. The block was built, was originally, as horse stable. There were 40 horses, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and through it was a kind of chimney, which was [INAUDIBLE] out of bricks. I mean a s-- a brick structure with two chimneys, which could have been fed with wood, or could have been used as heating from both sides. Heating it wasn't, always. Somebody was beaten-- was beaten simply that he put his head into the door of the chimney and was given, I mean, whatever he was allotted to. And there were bunks, triple bunks, and people were sleeping approximately six or eight abreast, with few blankets thrown over them, if that. Usually there was some kind of a straw mat. Sometimes there was no straw mat, just the wooden planks. Now, our block elders, meaning the oldest of the block, was training the guys how to respond when an SS man walks in, or when he calls simply to attention, how quickly they have to respond. They have to respond within 30 seconds, under 30 seconds, holding a cane in his hand. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: It went all night, training how to do this. He killed five people with a stick. It was, I mean, an exercise with utmost brutality, which simply was so unexpected. We suddenly-- I mean, I've seen something-- we were present to something which just was shattering. And at the same time, we were explained, don't make-- don't make big fuss about it. Just take a big whiff. What do you smell? Do you think that they are roasting a pig, or they are roasting a chicken there? No. They are roasting the Jews there. You are about 200 meters away from the gas chambers and from the crematoria. That's what you smell. That's the smell of the burned bodies. I believed it immediately. There were people simply who couldn't believe that such a thing is possible.

speaker: interviewee
text: Anyhow, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] there, about four days later, I met with Eva in a washroom, a block where there were faucets, and where sometimes water was running and where we were permitted to wash. Women, men. Didn't make any difference. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We didn't realize how lucky we are that there are women and children, and everybody in the same camp. We are alive. We didn't go through a selection. We went directly.

speaker: interviewer
text: Dr. Roden, we'll complete the story in a moment, but right now we're gonna take a break.
